data.title: Sibanye Stillwater: Deep Value And A Major EPS Rebound Coming (Upgrade)
data.description:
Thesis

This is my second time covering Sibanye Stillwater Limited (SBSW), a global company that produces large amounts of Platinum Group Metals, or PGMs, and is also expanding into battery minerals such as lithium. Sibanye operates mines and processing plants in South Africa, the United States, and Europe, and it's also involved in the cleaner energy sector.

In one year, it's had an incredible tech-like run, doubling in share value at one point since my Buy rating. Looking ahead to Sibanye’s earnings in a few days, I'm maintaining my bullish stance because I think there’s a reasonable case for continued optimism. Pricing has firmed up. And if management really stuck to the plan on costs and execution, then that discipline should show up in the numbers.

Sibanye Stillwater: Strategic Expansion and Market Leverage

Sibanye Stillwater is expected to share its full-year 2025 results on February 20. Analysts are modeling 10.37B in 2026. The core driver, as you know, is higher realized prices for PGMs and gold. There's a wide range of reasons prices have been rising, but let's not veer too far off into arguing things, like the destruction of wealth via fiat currencies, and just pinpoint it to the mainstream reasons: limited supplies and global political tensions. So as long as those constraints stick around, pricing should stay supportive.

I’m also confident, especially because of the U.S. PGM business. Last week, the U.S. Department of Commerce made a preliminary call that Russian palladium was being dumped into the U.S. They calculated a 132.83% dumping margin. If this plan actually goes through, the government could start charging import taxes at the same rate as soon as next week.

This is a big deal for the Stillwater mines in Montana. They’re basically the only meaningful U.S. PGM producer that isn’t tied to Russia. For years, subsidized metal flooded the market and crushed prices. Last year, Stillwater had to cut production in half, down to roughly 300,000 ounces. That hurt. But now, as you are well aware, prices are acting significantly better. Palladium has stabilized off the lows. And platinum has gone ballistic.

Last month, CEO Stewart said that even though near-term volatility is still there, the industry has now reached a higher minimum price level. That lines up with what Sibanye indicated last month. Stewart walked through their plan to squeeze more out of what they already have. Specifically, they’re focusing on higher-grade ore at Stillwater East and East Boulder. By concentrating on these higher-quality areas, the company may be able to become profitable again without needing to restart operations immediately at Stillwater West.

The antidumping ruling strengthens Sibanye’s position when negotiating with automakers too, who depend on PGMs to make catalytic converters. And because automakers still need these metals, Sibanye may have more leverage in pricing discussions. So if prices remain stable, this could help the company generate more predictable cash flow and potentially increase its annual EBITDA significantly.

Over in South Africa, it's no surprise that gold continues to be the company’s main profit driver. The Driefontein and Kloof mines, both of which have been operating far longer than originally planned, generated R3.6 billion in operating profit the last time I covered it. Now, Sibanye is shifting its focus to newer, more efficient operations. This also includes the Burnstone project. It’s a shallower, more mechanized mine, so it should be simpler to run. The plan is to produce around 130,000 ounces of gold a year.

At the same time, the company owns a stake in Durban Roodepoort Deep Gold (DRD). Through that, they reprocess old mine waste and pull gold out of tailings, which is basically leftover mining material. Taken together, these should give the gold division a pretty long runway, something around 40 to 50 years. But don’t take my word for it. Stewart said it’s

“one of our most successful investments, both from a production and a financial perspective… I think we invested about ZAR 2 billion to get our stake in DRD. Today, it's worth ZAR 27 billion to us.”

In South Africa, production is expected to remain steady at 1.5 million ounces by extending the life of existing operations (known as brownfield projects), including K4 and Siphumelele. These extensions require R4.4 billion in investment and are expected to keep production stable for the next 10 years. Meanwhile, management is targeting about 2.5% revenue growth. At the same time, they want to pull out $3 billion in costs. The way they plan to do that is with a hybrid operating model. In other words, big growth calls get centralized, but site teams still run day-to-day operations.

Between its operations in Montana and South Africa, Sibanye Stillwater has built a stronger presence in well-established mining regions. However, the company’s growth plans go far beyond these traditional mining locations. Europe’s Keliber lithium project in Finland shows how Sibanye Stillwater is planning ahead in the battery metals market. The project began phased commissioning in early 2026. The first phase focuses on mining and processing the ore to produce spodumene concentrate, which can already be sold profitably at current market prices. If lithium market conditions improve, the company has the option to move further and process the material into technical-grade lithium hydroxide or even battery-grade lithium hydroxide, depending on how strong the market recovery is.

The Keliber lithium project has been officially recognized as a strategic project by the European Union. It has a fully integrated setup, meaning lithium is mined and refined within the same project, something that's unique in Europe. This is especially important because more than 70% of global lithium processing currently takes place in China, something that I just discussed in more detail here. Once the project reaches full production capacity, it could produce enough lithium hydroxide to supply batteries for up to 3 million electric vehicles per year.

Just as important, the company is working to lock in cheaper, more reliable power. This dovetails nicely with Sibanye Stillwater’s broader commitment to renewable energy. On February 6, the company signed an agreement to secure 220 MW (megawatts) of solar and wind power. Then, on February 13, it entered into a renewable energy off-take agreement with NOA Group. Together, these steps are expected to move 30% of the company’s operations onto renewable energy by the end of the year.

SBSW: Deep Value with Upside Potential

SBSW’s cash flow performance does seem to suggest a very strong opportunity for investors right now. Historically, the company’s operating cash flow yield reached 13.43%. Right now, its P/OCF (blended price-to-operating-cash-flow) ratio is 7.45x. Roughly half of the estimated fair value ratio of 15.00x.

FAST Graphs is targeting a 42.34% estimated annual return via a projected 7.38% growth rate, which does a decent job of reflecting the company’s shift into lithium production through the Keliber project and stable PGM prices.

Looking at another way to value the company, its forward EV/EBITDA ratio is 5.71x. The average for the sector is 10.00x. That means the company is trading at about a 42.93% discount compared to similar companies, another nudge that you're getting a better deal than some of its peers.

Analysts’ consensus estimates support the outlook as well. They expect EPS to grow by 330.99% by 2026, alongside revenue increasing to 10.37B (from 7.79B last year).

Dilution isn't exactly immediate, but it's not impossible, and that high LT debt at 48.04% doesn't help. All in all, competition in the gold industry remains very strong, and any operational problems in South Africa could shorten what's expected to be a long production lifespan.

Final Verdict

I think that Sibanye Stillwater warrants a Strong Buy rating upgrade. The risk–reward balance looks tilted toward potentially strong gains moving forward. Revenue growth could lead to a big jump in EPS, and the company’s cash flow appears undervalued. Growth-seeking investors with commodity tolerance should buy; risk-averse: avoid.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Disc Medicine After The CRL: Risk Now Rests On APOLLO
data.description:
Thesis

As you know by now from the 21% stock slide, the FDA has issued a CRL to Disc Medicine Inc. (IRON) for bitopertin. However, on the surface, it appears to be more of a regulatory disagreement and not about whether the drug actually lowers its biochemical target. So it seems more about whether that biomarker itself reliably predicts real clinical benefit. The issue was in the subjectivity of whether patients showed a real lifestyle benefit. As I will explain, what it came down to was that different patients experienced or even started with different levels of sunlight exposure they were comfortable with. The result is that the FDA has deemed the AURORA and BEACON not sufficient and has indicated the need to see the results of the ongoing Phase 3 APOLLO study. Conveniently, in this press release, management has told us the APOLLO enrollment and progress are several months ahead of schedule. So we can expect to see results sometime before the end of the year.

So in my opinion, what this comes down to is the new endpoints included in the APOLLO trial and if they can convince the FDA that bitopertin leads to an actual lifestyle benefit for patients. I still think this is pretty difficult to do after going over the designs of the trials, as I'll explain. This is primarily the reason for going with a "Hold" on the stock. There was a lot of investor confidence baked into the price, with shares up about 32% on the 1-year chart before the sell-off yesterday. If the second time around also leads to a similar response from the FAD, in that there is just no direct way to statistically measure patient benefit due to the subjectivity, then there may be more downside for the stock. If investors are choosing to buy into the stock on this current dip, I would advise them to get to know the specific details of the APOLLO trial and understand the risk in the new endpoint.

Background

First, just for a bit of background, bitopertin is an oral inhibitor of glycine transporter 1 (GlyT1). GlyT1 is a membrane transporter we often see on developing red blood cells whose aim is to supply glycine for heme biosynthesis. You see, in erythropoietic protoporphyria (EPP), defective ferrochelatase activity is what leads to the accumulation of metal-free protoporphyrin IX (PPIX), which is a small phototoxic molecule that causes severe pain and tissue injury upon light exposure. So by limiting glycine uptake itself, bitopertin, in theory, should reduce the substrate supply for heme synthesis further upstream. Thus, this should decrease the production of PPIX. Mechanistically speaking, the approach is a bit complicated but would be classed as disease-modifying rather than symptomatic. It doesn’t just try to block the pain/inflammation, but instead it targets the biochemical pathway responsible for toxic PPIX buildup in erythroid cells. So, in simpler terms, it's best understood as bitopertin basically slows down heme production in our red blood cells by blocking glycine uptake. This is key, and as a result, this later reduces the formation of toxic protoporphyrin (PPIX) that causes light-induced pain in EPP.

FDA Complete Response Letter for Bitopertin

So overall, NDA initially relied primarily on data from the Phase 2 AURORA, a randomized and placebo-controlled trial. In addition, the BEACON open-label trials showed statistically significant reductions in whole-blood metal-free PPIX, which was the proposed surrogate endpoint for accelerated approval. Now, the FDA agreed on the first requirement for accelerated approval, which is that bitopertin clearly lowers PPIX levels. However, the accelerated approval also requires that the surrogate endpoint be reasonably likely to predict clinical benefit. This is what caught Disc Medicine out. This is what the FDA concluded that the trials did not demonstrate: a meaningful association between the percent reduction in PPIX and the improvement in sunlight-exposure-based endpoints. In terms of the exposure to sunlight, they used things like measured amounts of time in sunlight without pain or phototoxic reactions.

So, in other words, although the biomarker moved in the right direction and, in general, the biological rationale was strong, it was the clinical outcome measures used in both AURORA and BEACON that did not convincingly track with the biomarker changes. It's a bit of a disconnect and one that likely reflects challenges inherent to EPP trials. You will always have that high variability in sunlight exposure behavior with very subjective pain endpoints. The limited trial duration also didn't help.

You see, in patients with EPP, their symptoms depend pretty heavily on how much sunlight they choose, or I guess more accurately, how much they’re able to expose themselves to. That’s not something a trial can truly control. For example, two patients with the same PPIX reduction might in reality behave very differently. One might cautiously avoid the sun because years of conditioning and fear of pain have told them to do so. On the other hand, another patient may test their limits much more aggressively. So that means the time in sunlight or other endpoints ends up not being driven just by the drug effect, but also by psychology and lifestyle. That’s what the FDA didn’t like: the fact that there's this noise in the data about whether patients actually did better in real life. In my opinion, it’s going to be hard to show a tight correlation between that biochemical change in lower PPIX and a behavioral/experiential outcome of more sun tolerance for Disc Medicine.

Cause for Investor Concern

Another area I see cause for concern is in pain and phototoxic reactions. These are both subjective and episodic, so not really continuous measurements. Some patients may experience severe pain after just a small bit of exposure, and others just mild discomfort after longer exposure. Also, recall bias affects diary-based reporting here. When trials are quite short, we usually see them fail to capture enough real-world sunlight events to actually robustly demonstrate benefit. So even if bitopertin is doing what it is supposed to be doing biologically, which is lowering PPIX in red blood cells, the problem is that the trial endpoints may not move in parallel in a statistically convincing way. From the FDA’s perspective here, it introduces a lot of uncertainty about whether the biomarker they chose truly predicts meaningful clinical benefit, even when the mechanistic rationale behind the candidate is strong.

So the result of all this is that the FDA issued a CRL and has directed Disc to wait for results from the ongoing Phase 3 APOLLO trial. This trial is designed to directly link the reduction in PPIX we’ve seen to clinically meaningful improvements in light tolerance and symptoms. The FDA also indicated that positive APOLLO results could potentially support traditional approval. So that shifts the program from a surrogate-driven pathway to one more or less based on confirmatory clinical benefit.

Phase 3 APOLLO Trial

So that means the narrative now shifts towards the Phase 3 APOLLO trial for bitopertin. As you can imagine, it represents the most definitive test yet of the drug’s ability to actually improve meaningful outcomes in patients with EPP. However, I know what you’re thinking: Will this trial face the same endpoint problems as the earlier trials?

Well, the APOLLO trial is actually a substantial step up from the earlier Phase 2 programs. It’s a bit different from the smaller AURORA and BEACON studies, which were mainly focused on the surrogate biochemical endpoint of PPIX reduction and then looked to later collected sunlight exposure data with limited statistical power. APOLLO was actually designed from the outset with consultation from the FDA to address the past shortcomings. It’s a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled study, this time enrolling about 150 patients aged 12 and older. The patients have EPP, including X-linked protoporphyria (XLP), and are administered a single 60 mg daily dose of bitopertin over a full 6-month treatment period. Firstly, this is a much longer-duration design. So that's an improvement and should help the chance of detecting true differences in clinical outcomes.

Most importantly, however, APOLLO features co-primary endpoints. So we have the average monthly total time in sunlight without pain during the last month of treatment, which is a direct, patient-centric measure of benefit that reflects real quality-of-life improvement. We also have the percent change in whole blood metal-free PPIX after 6 months, which should allow the trial to simultaneously confirm both the clinical benefit and the biological effect. These endpoints have been agreed upon with the FDA to make the trial all-around more statistically robust and to better address regulatory expectations about linking PPIX reduction to decent lifestyle outcomes.

So the key here is that APOLLO directly measures pain-free sunlight exposure. This is the big improvement over earlier trials, where subjective reports and variable sunlight exposure limited the FDA’s interpretability. Enrollment is ongoing but set to be completed soon in March 2026. Management mentioned strong patient and physician interest and topline data to be expected sometime in 4Q26. The company also mentioned they have plans to use those results to support a traditional approval pathway and respond to CRL with an updated regulatory decision expected around mid-2027.

Valuation

From a valuation perspective, one thing the selloff did do was make shares look a bit more attractive at current levels. Disc Medicine, however, still remains pretty difficult to assess using traditional earnings-based metrics since the company is still pre-revenue and loss-making. They have no meaningful price-to-earnings ratio. Instead, I feel the most relevant framework is balance sheet strength and enterprise value. In that sense, they hold a market cap of about $2.1 billion and an enterprise value of about $1.5 billion. This is reflective of the company holding a hefty cash position relative to its size. Disc Medicine has about $616 million in cash and only about $31 million in debt. So it would seem that they are exceptionally well capitalized for a clinical-stage biotech, which should give them a runway into 2029 and plenty of ability to complete the APOLLO Phase 3 trial. I don't see much near-term financing risk moving forward. We also saw a $200 million debt financing back in October.

Elsewhere, on relative valuation metrics, the stock trades around 3.4x price to book, which is slightly above the healthcare sector median but also pretty reasonable for a company whose primary assets are just cash and late-stage clinical programs. I also think the sharp stock drop post the CRL reduced the near-term approval probability rather than any solvency or development risk. In practical terms, the market is now valuing Disc for the most part as a quite well-funded Phase 3 company with a single key asset, but with caution around the soon-to-come confirmatory data. It leaves valuation highly sensitive to APOLLO outcomes, as we know. The bull case here would see the re-rating of the stock toward a commercial-stage multiple. On the flip side, the failure would force the market to view the company primarily as a cash-rich R&D platform with a very uncertain pipeline value.

Looking Ahead

So, going forward, look out for the expected enrollment completion of the APOLLO study sometime next month, but mainly the results of the trial, which should come in 4Q26. We need to take another look at the stock, then, specifically, the lifestyle benefits of those exposed to the sun post-treatment. If there is any kind of subjectivity or cause for variability in the lifestyle improvement data, then expect investor concern to show up in the stock price again.

data.title: ATN International: Deal Approval Risks Persist, But The Risk-Reward Still Favors Bulls
data.description:
ATN International, Inc. (ATNI) is a telecommunications and broadband infrastructure provider that serves rural and remote parts of the US and select Caribbean markets. The company owns and operates network assets, such as fiber, transport, subsea connectivity, and towers. Recently, ATNI also announced a tower portfolio divestiture with proceeds to be used as a deleveraging catalyst. And, I believe that this methodical strategy will continue to accumulate shareholder value in the long run. Given that the stock’s valuation still seems attractive, I reiterate my bullish stance on ATNI at these levels.

Deleveraging Telecom

ATN International, Inc. is a digital infrastructure and communications services operator. Concretely, their infrastructure is mostly focused on serving rural and remote areas within the US as well as parts of the Caribbean. ATNI was founded back in 1987 and is currently headquartered in Beverly, Massachusetts. I previously covered ATNI last October with a “Buy” rating, and since then the stock has risen by roughly 87.1% (excluding dividends). So, I thought it was worthwhile to write an update to my thesis.

As a quick recap, ATNI’s US businesses include an Alaska component with fiber and subsea connectivity across the state. ATNI also operates in the Caribbean with integrated telecom in select islands, plus business services. ATNI invests in networks, building or buying telecom infrastructure, becoming one of the primary providers in a territory.

After that, ATNI monetizes the network, growing the broadband subscribers and selling network capacity to other companies. Those B2B clients tend to be mobile carriers that need roaming coverage in rural areas or enterprise connectivity in certain markets. Overall, they prioritize investments where expected returns are attractive for building and upgrading telecom infrastructure like fiber, towers, subsea cables, and electronics.

Tower Sale: Deleveraging Catalyst

Having said that, their recent Q3 2025 reported once again corroborates that their strategy seems to be working. Revenues reached $183.2 million (+3% YoY) with operating income of $9.8 million (which does show their margins aren’t as great, unfortunately). Still, Cash from operations came in at $97.7 million for the first nine months of 2025, which is again slightly higher than the first nine months of 2024 with $97.4 million in CFOs. And, while this is essentially flat in terms of cash flows, my main concern is whether or not they can continue to meet interest payments. After all, the original risk still remains because leverage and interest expense remain meaningful.

Fortunately, their nine-month interest expense was only $36.4 million, which seems largely covered by their cash flows from operations. Also, during that same period, ATNI deployed approximately $60.9 million in CAPEX. So, at its core, ATNI remains a fully self-sustainable business, which largely derisks their investment thesis in my view. But, on top of that, by February 12, 2026, ATNI’s subsidiary called “Commnet” (plus some of Commnet’s own subsidiaries) agreed to sell a portion of their telecom infrastructure. Specifically, the deal included 214 towers in the US Southwest to an Everest Infrastructure Partners affiliate for up to $297 million in cash. Additionally, this deal sold their tower portfolio, with physical tower sites, ground leases, permits, site-level contracts, and the operating rights related to the towers.

Everest thinks the tower’s acquisition can increase revenue by adding tenants over time, and for ATNI, it makes sense as it can help them delever their balance sheet. And, in that sense, it’s worth noting they’ve made more progress on that front as well. ATNI’s total debt is now around $579.6 million, with a net debt ratio of 2.47x in Q3 2025, which is an improvement relative to 2.58x at the end of Q2 2025. So we can indeed see a direct relation between this recent tower sale announcement and their ongoing debt reduction. Additionally, some of those proceeds will be used for investment in existing operations and selective growth. ATNI expects an initial closing of the transaction in Q2 2026, with gross proceeds between $250 million and $270 million.

Valuation And Risk Analysis

Naturally, selling assets will have an impact on ATNI’s topline. The deal’s press release mentioned that, in exchange for more cash and better leverage, removing their tower portfolio will reduce revenues by around $5 million to $7 million. Similarly, operating income will decrease by roughly $4 million to $6 million. But, ATNI intends to use approximately $70 million of the deal’s initial proceeds to repay borrowings under the CoBank revolving credit facility. In my view, this is a high-value deleveraging target because revolvers like these are often tied to floating rates and can be relatively expensive. So, paying down this particular type of liability should have an exceedingly positive impact on their cost of capital in the long run as well.

In my opinion, this deal will be a major tailwind in 2026 and beyond. And this is probably why the market has become increasingly bullish on ATNI. However, the reality is that some risks remain in this transaction. Technically speaking, the deal will be finalized only after all relevant third parties consent. After all, many towers sit on leased land, and the lease may require the landlord’s consent to assign the lease to a new owner.

So, in theory, tower lease agreements could restrict assignment or require notice to tenants for changes in operations. And, if a meaningful number of consents are delayed or denied, the deal’s closing could be delayed significantly, or even blocked, which I would imagine would play badly with the stock price by extension. Additionally, remember that there’s another condition that must be fulfilled before the deal closes. The Hart-Scott-Rodino (HSR) waiting period expiration refers to the US antitrust pre-merger review process. The waiting period is commonly 30 days for the Federal Trade Commission (FTC) and the Department of Justice (DOJ) to review the notifications and approve/reject them.

Alternatively, regulators could also issue a second request, which would probably require a deeper investigation and considerably delay the transaction. But, despite those regulatory risks, I believe that a tower portfolio sale of this size isn’t likely to trigger a serious antitrust block. This type of concern usually plays a larger role in telecom mega-mergers. And, by comparison, ATNI’s current market cap is only around $430.7 million, so I doubt regulators see a major antitrust risk with this type of transaction. Lastly, I also like that ATNI has continued paying its quarterly dividends consistently. The most recent one was for $0.275 per share, which shows they can pursue their balance sheet deleveraging while also returning some cash to investors.

Lastly, I still think ATNI trades at slightly cheap valuation multiples despite its price appreciation since I last covered it. Their 10-Q from Q3 2025 shows its balance sheet had $106.6 million in cash and short-term investments. And, as previously mentioned, their total debt is now around $579.6 million. So, that way I estimate ATNI’s EV at roughly $903.7 million. At the same time, according to Seeking Alpha’s dashboard on ATNI, the company is projected to generate approximately $739.4 million in revenues this year. So that implies a 1.2 EV/S multiple, which is still very compelling compared to its sector’s median EV/S of 1.9.

Conclusion: Still Bullish Long-Term

Overall, I think that ATNI’s investment thesis remains intact. Of course, the upside potential is now more limited than when I first covered it. However, in the long run, I believe management’s deleveraging strategy and consistent dividends will continue to accumulate shareholder value for investors. Plus, at its current valuation multiples, the stock actually appears slightly undervalued relative to its peers. So, on balance, I feel it’s still justified to be bullish on ATNI at these levels. Just remember that it does have latent headline risks until its deal is finalized and regulators approve it, which is why I stick with only a “Buy” rating for now.

data.title: Edison International: Earnings Strength Amid Wildfire Overhangs (Upgrade)
data.description:
Utilities have taken flight in recent weeks. The catalyst? Sharply lower Treasury yields. The comparative interest rate trade now favors popular income plays in the equity space. In fact, on the year-on-year view, the Utilities Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLU) is now outperforming the S&P 500 ETF (SPY).

Within Utilities, one name has risen big just lately. Edison International (EIX) jumped from $61 to $71 in short order. That came after a bottoming/consolidation stretch from early 2025 to the beginning of this year. I was "Neutral" on EIX back in December 2022. The stock has been a wild ride since then but sports a total return of 27%--much of that coming just this month.

Today, I’m upgrading the stock to a "Buy." The technical breakout looks real, while the valuation is quite attractive. Edison International reports Q4 results after the close on Wednesday.

EIX Outperforming XLU, SPY YoY

Back in October, EIX reported a solid set of quarterly results. Q3 non-GAAP EPS of $2.34 topped the Wall Street consensus forecast of $2.18, while revenue of $5.75 billion, up 11% from the same period last year, was about in line with estimates. The company narrowed its 2025 core EPS guidance to $5.95-$6.20, versus the consensus expectation of $6.03%.

Shares traded just fractionally lower in the session that followed. The 1.2% dip was well within the 4.3% options straddle pricing. Looking ahead to this week’s report, the options market prices in a small 2.9% options-implied stock price swing when analyzing the at-the-money straddle expiring soonest after the Wednesday evening report. Shares of the $27.5 billion market cap Utilities sector firm next go ex-dividend on April 7, while implied volatility is low at 28.5%.

Looking back on the quarter that was, EIX delivered solid top- and bottom-line numbers, with core EPS gains driven primarily by Southern California Edison’s (SCE) revenue uplift from the 2025 general rate case final decision. The ruling approved roughly 91% of requested capex and raised base revenue to fund critical grid investments while keeping system average rates the lowest among major California investor-owned utilities.

Q3 Highlights

The management team confirmed a tighter EPS guidance range, now $5.95–$6.20, with reaffirmed 5%–7% core EPS CAGR through 2028 off a $5.84 baseline. Helping the growth angle is a refreshed four‑year capital plan of $28–$29 billion and a projected 7%–8% rate-base CAGR, with no need for new equity under the consolidated financing plan. The top line is seen increasing by just 1%-3% annually, but there were positive signs regarding overall load growth. On Dec. 11, Edison also hiked its quarterly dividend by 6% to $0.8775.

Now, EIX was downgraded by a pair of sell-side firms lately. Wells cut the stock to Underweight with a $59 target, citing the Eaton Fire liability overhang. The stock has only skyrocketed since. Then, UBS turned less upbeat, but that was merely on valuation.

On the earnings outlook, EPS growth is expected to be strong this year, with potentially more than $6 of non-GAAP per-share earnings. Out-year growth is less impressive, but EIX could achieve $6 in operating EPS by FY 28. On net, the sell-side has turned more upbeat, with nine upgrades compared to just a lone downgrade, per Seeking Alpha. Ranked No. 8 out of 115 in its sector on the Factor Grades metrics, profitability trends are very strong.

EIX: Revenue & Earnings Forecasts, EPS Revision Trends

On valuation, if we assume $6.15 of normalized non-GAAP EPS and apply its long-term average P/E of 13.33x, then shares should trade near $83. That makes this out-west utility attractive on valuation. Risks remain, though. Even if we apply a 10% discount to account for wildfire risk, the stock is still reasonably compelling on earnings.

EIX: Modest Valuation Multiples

Key risks for EIX include uncertainty regarding the wildfire compensation program—it's unclear what the average claim and the full-loss estimate will be. What’s more, there are unknowns about the management team’s plan to mitigate wildfire risk going forward. It’s also possible that natural disaster risk could result in credit downgrades in the months ahead.

Of course, higher Treasury yields could result in lower overall valuations in the Utilities sector.

Competitor Analysis

Looking ahead, corporate event data provided by Wall Street Horizon show a confirmed Q4 2025 earnings date of Wednesday, Feb. 18 AMC. No other volatility catalysts are seen on the calendar.

Corporate Event Risk Calendar

The Technical Take

With shares still decently cheap amid ongoing wildfire uncertainty in California, EIX’s technical situation is strong. Notice in the chart below that shares have surged from the low $60s to the low $70s on a volume uptick. Furthermore, the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph confirms the price breakout. But where might the stock climb to from here (and after the Q4 earnings report)?

Well, take a look at the wildfire-related gap from 13 months ago. That level is $77. A gap fill would absolutely make sense, so we could see a pause there. Above that, the previous highs near $88 could be a long-term target. On the downside, the rising 200-day moving average is way down at $56, so shares are a bit extended. The former uptrend resistance line is at $64, so I see that as support.

EIX: Massive Upside Move, $78 Resistance

The Bottom Line

I have a "Buy" rating on Edison International. The embattled California-based Electric Utilities company has a solid EPS growth outlook, while the valuation and technicals augur for further upside with the Q4 report on tap.

data.title: Microsoft: Cloud And AI-Driven Stability
data.description:
Introduction and Investment Thesis

I am bullish on Microsoft Corporation (NASDAQ: MSFT), driven by Azure and its cloud services. This AI segment is currently seeing about 40% growth indicating that AI adoption is on the rise. For example, MSFT has reported, as of Q2’2026, commercial remaining performance (Backlog) of $625 billion. Aproximately 45% of this backlog is from OpenAI, representing around $281.3 billion in spending over the next several years. Other non-OpenAI enterprise contracts account for 55% of the $650 billion, or around $344 billion. Already, non-OpenAI has grown by 28% YoY as of Q2’2026, suggesting a fundamentally impressive growth trajectory, which is a reason for my optimism.

Microsoft is one of the major hyperscalers driving AI, and Q2’2025 is a testament to this optimistic view of AI as it shifts from a feared AI bubble to real economic value. So far, AI has been capable of performing tasks of value, indicating that it is adding real value. Another example is that in 2026, over 39% of jobs had exposure to AI, which is higher than the originally expected 30%. This is to say that AI can increasingly automate tasks than was initially expected. Some notable changes include legal professions, which have increased from 95% to 63%; education from 11% to 49%; health practitioners, from 10% to 39%; and C-suite roles such as CEO, from 25% to 60%.

The company has been making intensive capital expenditures, such as $35 billion in the July-September 2025 period, and I think this aggressive expenditure explains why its YTD price return has lagged the S&P 500. However, looking at the YoY growth of the AI segment, ranging between in the 40% regions and the backlog, which presents a promising revenue stream, I believe the market is overreacting to the company's aggressive investment. Most interesting, the company's return on invested capital is currently at 26.09% and at a consistent uptrend, justifying the heavy investment. Even further, it's above the company’s WACC which is estimated at about 8.9%.

I will now dive deep into why I think AI and cloud services warrant a bullish potential on Microsoft.

Company Brief

Microsoft Corporation develops global software, devices, services, and solutions. Its segments include Productivity and Business Processes (Dynamics, LinkedIn, and Microsoft 365), Intelligent Cloud (SQL Server, Nuance, Azure, and GitHub), and Personal Computing (Xbox, Surface, Windows, Bing, Edge ), as well as other product sales to distributors, OEMs, and retailers. The company was incorporated in 1975, and it is headquartered in Redmond, Washington.

Cloud and AI Growth Is Imminent

Recall that in my previous explanations, I mentioned that Microsoft’s AI business is soaring, which I am relating to the AI boom. One way I am looking at the AI boom is through the lens of capital spending on AI, which is expected to increase to $527 billion in 2026, up from $465 billion in Q3’2025. This has driven AI stocks, especially AI hyperscalers, whose prices are estimated to have declined from 80% to 20% as investments start generating revenue benefits, which is investor confidence.

As such, this expenditure is expected to continue through 2030 as AI adoption soars. In fact, if capital investment supporting AI-related data centers remains around $3 trillion and $8 trillion by 2030, in an accelerated demand scenario, data center infrastructure capital spending is likely to reach around $7.9 trillion by 2030. If demand for AI is relatively constrained, it might reach $5.2 trillion; in a more constrained scenario, it might reach around $5.2 trillion.

Now, with this perspective, I am connecting the dots on why AI programs such as Microsoft Azure are used by companies like Air India to automate customer support, and why they have achieved over 97% query automation to date, representing over 4 million queries. This is a testimony that, as programs such as MSFT’s Azure Storage innovate to a unified intelligent platform, it is likely to experience soaring market access. These innovations have continued to help OpenAI handle unprecedented workloads available in all enterprises.

While the market is punishing stocks for their heavy AI expenditure, MSFT is showing impressive returns and solid growth as mentioned above and I think it's about time the market appreciates the positivity.

Comparative Analysis

A comparative analysis of MSFT with its peers, NVIDIA Corporation (NASDAQ: NVDA) and Broadcom Inc. (NASDAQ: AVGO), from varied angles demonstrates Microsoft’s consistency. For example, from a revenue perspective, MSFT’s revenue consistency is evident in its leading revenue trajectory from 2021 to the current revenue, which is in the upwards of $305.45 billion. MSFT’s revenues are a distant lead over NVDIA’s, at $187.14 billion and AVGO's at $63.89 billion, respectively, revealing that MSFT has a solid market position. The revenues have been less volatile compared to NVIDIA’s, which is relatively volatile. As such, I think this consistency creates some degree of investor confidence backed by the strong fundamentals discussed above, which could drive bullish momentum.

Even more interesting, MSFT’s EBITDA leads its peers at $191.39 billion compared to NVDA’s $119.09 billion and AVGO’s $34.71 billion. MSFT has maintained a consistent, gradual EBITDA growth trend, with its current EBITDA being almost 2x that of its close competitor NVDA. This is a testament to MSFT’s stronger absolute profitability compared to its peers, which provides another reason to be optimistic.

From the margin perspective, which stands at 62.66%, slightly below NVIDIA’s 63.64%, MSFT’s EBITDA margin has maintained a consistent upside trajectory compared to NVIDIA’s, which has been highly volatile, making MSFT a more stable investment than NVDA.

Important to note, I think the slightly lower margin of MSFT compared to NVDA is due to the former’s solid R&D expenditure compared to the others where it spends more than double its closest peer.

This clearly shows that MSFT is sacrificing profitability supremacy for long term investment. Either way, its profitability appears to be the most stable and improving gradually despite its highest investment which is a solid display in my view.

Earnings highlights

MSFT released its Q2 2026 on 28th January, 2026, which demonstrates that its AI and cloud services are gaining traction rapidly with a trailing net profit of $119.26 billion, a 28.58% YoY growth. This represented a 3-year all-time high net income growth rate confirming this tailwind.

The revenue growth rate by segment shows that the Intelligent Cloud segment is growing at 28.82%, almost 2x that of productivity and business processes, at 15.89% and a distant high from the personal computing segment, which is at -2.74%. With this grounded top-line foundation and the solid EBITDA margin, I believe this company’s strong fundamentals can’t be stated any better.

In FY2025, the company beat its earnings estimate by 1.62% to $13.64. This was around a 15.59% EPS YoY growth rate, but in FY2026, the tailwinds discussed above are estimated to drive the earnings to around $16.73, representing 22.64% growth. I believe this strong execution capability will likely persist in FY2026, and this could be another consecutive FY in which MSFT beats analysts’ earnings estimates driven by AI and Cloud services.

To wrap up this section, let’s see how its P/S multiple compares to its peers, NVDA and AVGO. Right now, MSFT’s P/S multiple is 10.06x, which is below NVDA’s at 24.59x and AVGO’s 25.11x.

I consider this an undervaluation with MSFT’s market dominance reflected in the top-line performance above. Well, the trailing revenue growth rate for MSFT is 16.67% which is lower than NVDA’s 65.22% and AVGO’s 23.87%. While this shows that the two peers have stronger growth rates, I believe the market ignores MSFT’s maturity signaled by its less volatile revenue and margins. I think MSFT warrants a P/S multiple in the region of 15x-20x.

Investment Risk: AI Infrastructure Constraint

Microsoft is facing significant execution risk from AI infrastructure demands, amid recent concerns about the profitability of AI investments, which is driving a bearish move. According to the Q2 2026 report, the growth of data centers is highly dependent on the availability of predictable energy, permitted and buildable land, servers, networking supplies, and graphics units, among other components. So far, MSFT has committed over $155.1 billion to uncommenced data center leases through 2031. Therefore, even though MSFT cloud revenue has reached over $51.5 billion, reducing the gross margin to around 67% due to continued AI investments, the company faces profitability pressure and limited GPU supplies, which could prolong its forecasts.

Conclusion

I am reiterating my bullish stance on MSFT, driven by a market tailwind, especially around AI investment, which is matched by impressive uptake that promises to conduct tasks valued at over $4.5 trillion. The exposure is around 39% which means there is a substantial untapped market yet, which is why I think this company has bullish momentum leeway.

data.title: TC Energy: A Great Natural Gas Play
data.description:
Introduction

TC Energy (NYSE/TSX: TRP) is one of the few midstream companies that I have not yet reviewed, despite being heavily bullish on the sector. The energy infrastructure landscape underwent a significant structural shift in 2025, a period defined by the rising electrification demands and the prioritization of capital discipline. TC Energy, having just completed its transformative spinoff of its liquids pipelines, could be a very interesting and focused play on the rising demand for natural gas.

TRP has emerged as a company focused on the two highest-growth segments of the energy transition, natural gas and nuclear power. This strategic narrowing culminated in a series of record-breaking operational performances and financial results that have blown all expectations. I will dive into why I believe TRP could be a great play ahead.

Current Dynamics

I believe that 2025 was a successful transition period for TC Energy as it shifted from capital-heavy construction into an operational optimization phase. Q4 served as a primary catalyst for renewed investor confidence, in my mind, revealing that the energy firm exceeded key indicators despite the inherent market volatility. Quarterly revenue came in at C$4.1B, slightly beating the consensus of C$4.08B. This top-line performance represents a robust 16.5% Y/Y increase. This has been heavily linked to the increased utilization of the company’s natural gas pipeline systems, which saw unprecedented flow rates due to seasonal demand and new capacity additions. The company also posted an EPS beat of 1.85% to reach C$0.98.

On a full-year basis, TC Energy generated $11B in comparable EBITDA, which is a $1B increase over 2024 and a 9% annual growth despite removing the historical contribution from the Keystone system. This was largely thanks to higher transportation rates on the Columbia Gas and NGTL systems, alongside the successful ramp-up of the Southeast Gateway pipeline in Mexico.

I believe that it is important to dive deeper segment-wise. Right now, TRP moves roughly 30% of the natural gas used in North America, positioning it as a crucial link in the energy supply chain. The Canadian system set an all-time delivery record of 33.2 Bcf/d, representing a 5% increase over the previous year. The NGTL system serves as the primary gathering network of the Western Sedimentary Basin and saw its receipts average 15.5 Bcf/d in the fourth quarter, a 2% rise Y/Y.

Another highlight of the year has been the operational integration of the Coast GasLink pipeline. The project moved through final environmental reclamation and began facilitating the first LNG shipments from Canada’s west coast in mid-2025. Though the project ran way over budget, its role in unlocking global markets for Canadian gas provides a foundational long-term cash flow stream for the company.

On the US gas side, it reported an even more aggressive growth profile than its Canadian counterpart, with average daily flows of 29.6 Bcf/d, a 9.5% increase compared to the fourth quarter of 2024. In late January, the US network achieved a record delivery of 39.9 Bcf, thanks to the deliveries to LNG export facilities. The segment was also boosted by the successful settlement of the Columbia Gas Rate Case, which increased rates relative to pre-filed levels and contributed $188MM in incremental EBITDA. TRP continues to leverage its “in-corridor” expansion strategy in the US, placing projects like the $500MM VR expansion and the $700MM WR project into service in late 2025.

The completion of the Southeast Gateway pipeline also marked a definitive turning point for the segment as EBITDA from the Mexico segment rose by 70% Y/Y, reaching $163MM. The pipeline currently transports 2.7 Bcf/d, which accounts for around 20% of Mexico’s total natural gas demand.

I also want to quickly touch on TC Energy’s power and energy solutions business, which is primarily focused on its 48.3% interest in Bruce Power, as it provides a unique differentiator from other midstream energy companies. With the current environment of energy companies, the nuclear portfolio has become a cornerstone of the company’s low-risk investment thesis. Apparently, for every day that Bruce Power is available, it generates roughly $1MM in incremental revenue for the company. For the full year, availability reached 91%, and management is expecting to maintain this high 90% range through 2026.

Valuation

I believe that right now, TC Energy is priced at a growth premium, with the market increasingly viewing the company as a specialized infrastructure play positioned to benefit from surging power demand. It currently has a P/E Non-GAAP of 24.99, the second highest in its peer group behind Williams Companies (WMB) at 34.42. On an EV/EBITDA basis, it currently stands at 17.17, again the second highest of its peers, and places it at a premium over peers like Enbridge that arguably have a higher moat than TRP. I’ll even say that the two major Canadian midstream companies have followed two very different strategies that have diverged significantly, whereas ENB is going through extreme diversification, while TC has streamlined itself to focus on natural gas and nuclear power. The company’s current dividend yield is standing at 3.85%, which is lower than the broad midstream market but still is a fair entry point compared to the broad market. And though many could say that TRP is currently too expensive, I would say that the market underappreciates the nuclear kicker of the company as it provides a unique, low-risk, zero-emission growth vector that justifies a utility-like valuation premium.

TC Energy potentially has the best growth prospects in the midstream industry thanks to its specialization in natural gas and nuclear. Management has provided a visible growth runway with a target of 5% to 7% annual comparable EBITDA growth through 2028. I believe that the main reason has been the wakening of a natural gas super cycle in North America, as demand is set to increase by 45 Bcf/d by 2035, driven by LNG exports, coal to gas conversions and industrial demand. As TRP is transporting 30% of the continent’s natural gas, it is uniquely positioned to capture this through brownfield expansions. The rising electricity needs from data centers and AI are driving a massive need for natural gas-fired power generation. TRP’s infrastructure is located near 60% of projected US data center growth, with recent non-binding open seasons for the Columbia Gas and Crossroads systems being highly oversubscribed.

Risks

Despite the company’s efforts to deleverage, it still has a high debt-to-EBITDA ratio of 4.8, as TRP still maintains a substantial debt load of nearly $60B. This could cause the company to sustain higher funding costs for its $21.4B project backlog.

The company also has a premium valuation, which leaves limited room for disappointment in earnings or project execution. And this has been highlighted by the Coastal GasLink project, which saw costs balloon from $6.2B to $14.5B, serving as a warning of the risks inherent in large-scale energy infrastructure.

Conclusion

TC Energy finished 2025 in its strongest operational position in nearly a decade, in my opinion. The company is currently aligned with the most resilient demand drivers on the continent in terms of energy. The insatiable demand from data centers and the growing role of NA as a global LNG supplier provide a clear runway for TC Energy. It is richly valued, though, and that is why I give it a small Buy rating.

data.title: REITs Rally As Rates Retreat
data.description:
Real Estate Weekly Outlook

U.S. equity markets slumped this past week, while Treasury yields pulled back sharply, as a confluence of cooling macro data and mixed corporate earnings results reinforced the value rotation and revived expectations of easing monetary policy. Yields tumbled after the critical Consumer Price Index report showed a continued cooldown in inflation to four-year lows, quelling concerns that tariff increases would lead to sustained upward price pressures. Labor market data landed squarely in “Goldilocks” territory – firm enough to quiet recession fears but soft enough to avoid reigniting rate anxiety – while softer retail sales underscored a gradual cooling in consumer momentum. Together, these forces accelerated the value rotation push, benefiting cyclicals and income-oriented sectors at the expense of AI-exposed tech sectors.

Posting declines for the fourth time in the past five weeks, the S&P 500 slipped 1.2% on the week, erasing its year-to-date gains. Smaller-cap and rate-sensitive segments outperformed for a second straight week, continuing the "value rotation" dynamic that has been the key equity market theme of early 2026. The Small-Cap 600 and Mid-Cap 400 posted more modest declines this week, maintaining their year-to-date gains at about 8%. The tech-heavy Nasdaq 100 slumped 1% - now down about 5% from its recent-highs – amid ongoing concern about the AI impact on the software industry itself. Buoyed by the rate retreat, real estate equities posted sizable outperformance this week as earnings season kicked into high gear with strong reports from data center, senior housing, and retail REITs, offsetting some weakness from office and multifamily REITs. The Equity REIT Index rallied 2.5% on the week, with 14 of 20 property sectors in positive territory. The Housing Index advanced 1% as strength from homebuilders on rate optimism and M&A news helped offset sharp pressure on brokerage firms.

In bond markets, Treasury yields retreated across the curve for a second straight week as traders responded to the deluge of macro data by tempering expectations of an imminent Fed rate cut but pricing in a higher quantity of cuts later this year. Closing at the lowest levels since August 2022, the 2-Year Treasury Yield dipped 9 basis points to close at 3.41%. The 10-Year Treasury Yield plunged 16 basis points to 4.05%, down sharply from the intraday highs above 4.30% in late January and back within range of its three-year lows of 3.95% seen last October. While traders remain skeptical of a March rate cut – with odds dipping to 10% this week from 185 last week – swaps markets now price in a median Fed Funds Rate of 3.11% at the end of 2026, implying 2.5 rate cuts for the year, a considerably more dovish outlook compared to the 2.2 rate cuts implied last week. In commodities and currency markets, energy prices continued to moderate as temperatures warmed amid the coldest winter in decades for many East Coast cities. Crude Oil prices declined 1% to around $61/barrel, while Natural Gas spot prices tumbled for a third straight week. Gold prices advanced nearly 2% for a second straight week, while Bitcoin rebounded from two-year lows, both stabilizing after a late-January selloff across the trendy "dollar debasement" plays.

Real Estate Economic Data

Below, we recap the most important economic data points over this past week affecting the residential and commercial real estate marketplace.

More good news on the inflation front. The critical Consumer Price Index this week surprised to the downside once again, despite some snap-back effects from the shutdown-impacted reports in prior months and some historical upward bias in recent early-year inflation reports. Headline CPI increased 2.39% from a year ago in January – the second-coolest annual increase of the past four years – and below consensus estimates of 2.50%. Core CPI – which excludes food and energy – increased 2.50% from a year ago, down from 2.64% in December, which marked the lowest level of core inflation since March 2021. For context, the Fed Funds rate in March 2021 was at the zero lower bound and remained there for the subsequent year, until Core CPI surged to 6.5% in March 2022. Perhaps most importantly, the CPI-ex-Shelter Index – the metric we follow most closely, given the substantial distortions from the heavily weighted (and arguably miscalculated) shelter component – cooled to 2.04% in January, the lowest since June.

We've highlighted for several years how the CPI's heavy weighting in the shelter category – combined with a 12-18 month sampling lag for these housing categories – resulted in a substantial understating of inflation as rents and home prices surged in 2021 and 2022, and a subsequent overstating of inflation since 2023 as real-time housing inflation has cooled substantially to near zero. For that reason, we compile a Real Time CPI using the Zillow Home Price Index (ZVHI) and Zillow Rent Index (ZORI) as a substitute for the BLS' indexes for primary rent and owner's equivalent rent. This adjusted CPI shows that inflation has averaged 2.0% since late 2023. While the Shelter component in CPI has moderated in recent months, it remained the most significant upside contributor in January, responsible for 39% of the monthly increase and 45% of the annual increase in the CPI index. Besides shelter, airfares jumped +6.5% m/m, contributing roughly 30% of the monthly CPI increase, while hospital services (+0.9%) also added modest upward pressure. Offsetting these gains, energy prices were a meaningful drag, led by a -3.2% m/m decline in gasoline, which alone subtracted roughly half of the month’s CPI increase. Used cars and trucks (01.8%) continued to deflate, while motor vehicle insurance and fuel oil also provided downside offsets.

Following a wave of surprisingly cool labor market data last week, the BLS Nonfarm Payrolls report - which was delayed a week by a second (brief) government shutdown in early February - surprised to the upside in January, pushing back on concerns of a significant deterioration in labor market conditions. Employers added 130k jobs in January, the strongest month of hiring since December 2024 and well above consensus expectations of +66k. The prior two months, however, were revised lower by 17k combined, while the annual benchmark revisions made sizable downward revisions to employment figures in 2024 and 2025, totaling -862k - slightly worse than the consensus of a -850k downward revision. For January, private payroll gains were notably strong at +172k, offset by a -42k dip in government employment. The Unemployment Rate edged down to 4.3%, while the Participation Rate improved slightly. Average Hourly Earnings rose 0.4% in January, bringing full-year wage growth to 3.7%, still outpacing inflation but consistent with the gradual cooling trend. Sector-level details continued to show a bifurcated labor market, with strength concentrated in defensive and service-oriented industries (educational and healthcare, in particular), while cyclical sectors have softened in recent months.

Equity REIT & Homebuilder Week In Review

Best & Worst Performance This Week Across the REIT Sector

Real estate earnings season kicked into high gear this week, with reports from roughly three dozen REITs, which pushed us to the halfway point of earnings season. As discussed in our REIT Earnings Halftime Report (available to our Investing Group subscribers), aided by favorable "goldilocks" macro data trends – cooling inflation alongside "good enough" labor market data – REITs have delivered notable earnings season outperformance thus far over the S&P 500. Underlying REIT results have been solid, with no major bombshells and several upside surprises. 51% of REITs reported 2025 FFO beating their full-year outlook, with better trends in underlying property-level metrics. Data Center, Senior Housing, and Retail REITs have been the upside standouts. AI-fueled demand has kept data center fundamentals tight despite record new supply. Senior Housing has remained red-hot. Retail REIT results have also been a pleasant upside surprise. Limited new supply has kept occupancy rates near record highs, and rent spreads in double digits despite a slight demand moderation. Apartment, Office, and Commercial Mortgage REITs have been the notable laggards. Multifamily rents softened in late 2025, particularly in the Sunbelt. Office REIT results showed progress at the property level but lingering headwinds on bottom-line FFO from balance sheet repair. Results from Commercial mREIT have been a setback after several encouraging quarters, as pockets of distress lingered in office, multifamily, and lab space loans, stretching dividend coverage. Below, we discuss the notable highlights and lowlights of the past week.

Data Center: Beginning with the upside standouts this week, Equinix (EQIX)—the largest data center REIT – surged 13% after it delivered a decisive earnings beat, boosted its dividend by 10%, and introduced a 2026 outlook that meaningfully exceeded expectations. Fueled by robust enterprise, hyperscale, and AI-driven demand, EQIX reported normalized AFFO growth of 9.5% in 2025 – matching its prior guidance – and forecasts 10.4% AFFO growth in 2026, well above consensus expectations and a sharp step-up from the 5% growth profile outlined at its June Analyst Day. Bookings surged 42% year-over-year to $474M, supported by strong enterprise and AI-related demand, with an additional $170M of pre-sales exiting 2025 and another ~$100M signed early in 2026. Consistent with the report last week from its peer Digital Realty (DLR), pricing power remained very strong despite booming supply. EQIX reported same-store recurring revenue growth of 6% - the strongest since late 2023 - while MRR per cabinet increased 8%. While interconnection net adds of 4,700 came in modestly below recent averages, EQIX expects to deliver approximately 18,000 cabinets in 2026 and nearly 26,000 in 2027. Iron Mountain (IRM) surged 15% after reporting a stellar quarter for its growing data center segment -which now accounts for roughly a third of its revenues - and surprisingly strong double-digit revenue growth in its legacy business storage segment. Fueled by 30% revenue growth in its data center segment, IRM delivered full-year FFO growth of nearly 14% in 2025 and guided to growth of another 11% in 2026, expecting at least 25% revenue growth in its data center segment based on its current backlog.

Senior Housing: Welltower (WELL) - the largest senior housing owner in the country and top-performing REIT on a rolling five-year basis – surged 8% this week after reporting another stellar quarter and providing a very strong 2026 outlook, fueled by continued outperformance in its Senior Housing Operating Portfolio (“SHOP”). WELL reported full-year FFO growth of 22.5% in 2025 – above its prior guidance of 18.1% – and forecasts these robust trends to continue in 2026 with an outlook calling for FFO growth of 16.6% this year. Senior Housing NOI grew 21.5% in 2025 – topping its prior guidance – and WELL forecasts growth of 18.0% in 2026 (VTR: 15%, DOC: 13%), driven by further occupancy gains (+350 bps expected) and sustained pricing momentum (5% RevPOR growth). WELL continues to be among the most active REITs on the external-growth front, highlighting $5.7B of investments closed or under contract year-to-date, the launch of a $2.5B senior housing private fund, and the $1.3B sale of a portion of its Integra portfolio, further simplifying the portfolio toward a pure-play SHOP strategy. Sabra Healthcare (SBRA) – which owns a mix of senior housing and skilled nursing assets – rallied 6% this week after reporting in-line fourth-quarter results but provided a forecast indicating 5%+ FFO growth in 2026. While its SHOP segment remains the outperformer, the skilled nursing business continued to strengthen at the margins in late 2025 via occupancy and rent coverage improvement.

Medical Office: Sticking in the healthcare space, Healthcare Realty (HR) – the second-largest Medical Office Building ("MOB") REIT – surged 6% this week after it reported better-than-expected results, driven by a late-year acceleration in leasing activity. HR delivered full-year FFO growth of 3.5% for 2025 – topping its prior forecast of 2.3% – which snapped a stretch of three straight annual FFO declines. Strong leasing volume in Q4 drove a +100 bps year-over-year increase in occupancy, with base rents up 4.7%. Citing a favorable supply-demand balance from a sharp pullback in new development, HR delivered its strongest pace of full-year NOI growth since before the pandemic. Its core MOB segment delivered 4.8% NOI growth in 2025 – above its prior forecast of 4.4% – which was consistent with the fourth-quarter MOB outperformance reported earlier in earnings season from Welltower and Healthpeak. Guidance calls for flat FFO growth in 2026 at the midpoint of its initial range, as solid NOI growth of 4% is offset by some headwinds from the $1B+ in asset sales over the past year, which helped it reset its leverage to a very healthy 5.4x Debt/EBITDA multiple from 6.4x at the start of the year.

Net Lease: Boosted by the pullback in interest rates, the net lease sector delivered a very strong week as results from seven REITs showed an acceleration in acquisition activity – and in bottom-line FFO growth – as investment spreads become more accretive. Getty Realty (GTY) – which focuses on gas stations and other automotive-related real estate – jumped 8% after reporting solid fourth quarter results highlighted by a notable acceleration in acquisition activity. GTY completed $269M of investments in 2025 (up 28% from 2024), including $135M in Q4 at a 7.9% cap rate. WP Carey (WPC) – the second-largest net lease REIT – gained 4% after reporting solid results that capped off an active 2025 as it completed a multi-year strategic portfolio repositioning to focus more exclusively on industrial real estate. Fueled by embedded contractual escalators and robust investment activity, WPC reported full-year FFO growth of 5.7% in 2025 and forecast FFO growth of 4.2% for full-year 2026. Essentials Property (EPRT) – which has been the top-performing net lease REIT on a rolling five-year basis – gained 1% after reporting sector-leading FFO growth in 2025 and raised its already upbeat outlook for 2026. Agree Realty (ADC) and National Retail (NNN) – the third- and fourth-largest net lease REITs – each gained about 3% after reporting similarly solid results that topped prior guidance. On the downside, Four Corners (FCPT) – which focuses on restaurants – dipped 3% after reporting modestly disappointing results amid a slight uptick in missed rents.

Strip Center: Results from a half-dozen strip center REITs this week showed a continuation of robust retail real estate fundamentals in late 2025 and into 2026, with the sector on pace for a 15th-straight quarter of double-digit leasing spreads. Kimco (KIM) – the largest strip center REIT – rallied 4% this week after reporting solid fourth-quarter results, highlighted by a record-high occupancy rate driven by strength across both its anchor and small-shop segments. Leasing trends remained healthy, highlighted by 2.7M SF of leasing activity in Q4 2025 and blended cash leasing spreads of +13.8% (TTM: 13.0%) – its 10th straight quarter of double-digit spreads. Brixmor (BRX) gained 3% – which owns a regionally diverse portfolio focused on grocery-anchored centers – gained 3% after reporting similarly solid results, highlighted by an impressive +24% blended spread (an acceleration from its TTM rate of +20%), led by strong in-line leasing that pushed its overall leased rate to 95.1%, just below record highs for the company. InvenTrust (IVT) – which focuses on grocery-anchored centers in the Southeast – gained 1% this week after reporting solid results and hiking its dividend by 5%. On the downside, Acadia Realty (AKR) – which owns a high concentration of street-level retail in urban markets – dipped 2% this week as investors focus on a less-than-clean earnings mix distorted by some special items related primarily to its Albertsons investment. Urban Edge (UE) – which focuses on the Northeast region with a skew towards large power centers – slipped 1% this week despite hiking its dividend by 11% alongside an in-line earnings report. Federal Realty (FRT) - which owns a more "mixed-use" portfolio with 10% of its portfolio in residential and office - also slipped 1% this week as strong retail leasing was partially offset by softer trends in its other segments.

Apartment: Results from Apartment REITs have been modestly disappointing, with results showing the early-year reacceleration in multifamily rent growth completely reversed in the back half of the year, even in the previously hot Northeast and Midwest markets. UDR Inc. (UDR) – the fifth-largest apartment REIT with the most regionally diverse portfolios among its peers – gained 1% this week after reporting mixed Q4 results but provided a relatively constructive outlook for 2026. UDR reported blended rent growth of -2.1% in Q4 (-7.2% on new leases and +2.7% on renewals) – the weakest among the five apartment REITs – but UDR emphasized that it was aggressively discounting rents toward a high occupancy strategy, which did indeed lift its same-store occupancy rate to 96.9% – up 20 basis points from a year ago. At the regional level, the West was the clear relative outperformer, delivering +0.9% blended growth. The Northeast and Mid-Atlantic saw modest blended declines (-1.5% and -2.5%), while the Southeast and Southwest experienced the sharpest softness, driven by double-digit new-lease declines and flat renewal growth. Encouragingly, turnover declined meaningfully across all regions year-over-year, consistent with trends observed across the sector. UDR noted that blended lease growth improved steadily through Q4 and turned positive in January (+1.0%) and is guided to +1.5–2.0% in 1Q26. Sunbelt-focused Independence (IRT) slipped 1.5% after reporting mixed results, as a surprising reacceleration in blended spreads in late 2025 - the only REIT to report a sequential uptick – was offset by a muted FFO outlook for 2026 on higher interest expense and lower development yields.

Office: On the downside this week, results from a half-dozen office REITs showed that while property-level trends have inflected positively—evidenced by improved leasing volumes, modest occupancy gains, and stabilizing in-place rents—bottom-line profitability remains constrained by elevated leverage, higher interest expense, and balance sheets still healing from years of value erosion. West Coast-focused Kilroy (KRC) dipped 9% after reporting a transitional 4Q25, as decent leasing volumes were overshadowed by a meaningful reset in 2026 earnings expectations. KRC reported that its full-year FFO declined -8.5% in 2025 – below its prior guide - and its 2026 outlook calls for a steep -20.2% FFO decline in 2026. Leasing volumes were the bright spot, with 827K SF signed during the quarter (well above its TTM average of around 475k SF), but cash leasing spreads deteriorated to -27.1% in the quarter, its worst on record. Douglas Emmett (DEI) – which focuses exclusively on the L.A. and Honolulu office markets – slipped 5% after reporting similar trends. DEI reported a full-year FFO dip of -15.2% in 2025 and forecasts another down year in 2026, with FFO expected to decline by -2.1% this year. DEI signed 906k SF in Q4 2025, up nearly 14% year-over-year and modestly above its TTM average, but cash leasing spreads dipped -10.1% – its fourth straight quarter with double-digit spread declines. Sunbelt-focused Highwoods (HIW) dipped 10% after decent Q4 results and a constructive 2026 outlook were offset by a subsequent $300M equity issuance. HIW reported a full-year FFO decline of -3.6% in 2025 but forecasts a return to growth in 2026, with expectations of 1.7% FFO growth this year. Vornado (VNO) – the second largest office REIT – slipped 4% after it reported a mixed Q4 2025, with improving fundamentals in its core New York office portfolio offset by some property-specific headwinds.

Industrial: Also among the laggards this week, LXP Industrial (LXP) dipped 7% this week after reporting mixed fourth-quarter results, as a late-year rebound in leasing activity wasn't enough to offset a very slow start to the year amid tariff uncertainty. LXP reported an FFO decline of -1.6% in 2025 – below its prior forecast of -0.8% – its seventh annual FFO decline in the past nine years. None of the six largest industrial REITs has reported a single annual FFO decline during this period. Despite a solid leasing pace in Q4, LXP ultimately leased 5M SF of space in 2025 – down 26% from last year - and has 41% of its 2026 rent rollover renewed compared to the roughly 55% at this time last year. LXP commented that "it continues to be a real challenge to get deals done, lots of RFP traffic... really just getting them across the finish line." As a result, same-store occupancy dipped to 97.3% at year-end, down 220 basis points from last year. LXP – which focuses primarily on single-tenant triple net leased properties - reported disappointing 8.8% leasing spreads, weighed down by several fixed-rate renewals. (22.6% excluding these fixed renewals). STAG Industrial (STAG) declined 3% despite reporting a solid quarter of leasing activity and a corresponding boost to its same-store occupancy. STAG reported full-year FFO growth of 6.7% in 2025 - well above its prior guidance of 5.4%. STAG has addressed 69% of its 2026 expirations – roughly in line with its pace at this time last year – and expects cash leasing spreads of 20% in 2026, only a modest step down from the 25% spreads achieved in 2025. STAG provided a relatively muted outlook for 2026, however, with expectations of 2.2% FFO growth this year, reflecting an occupancy headwind of 120 basis points (vs. -100 bps previously).

Commercial mREITs: While still early in earnings season for the sector, results across the commercial mortgage space have been disappointing thus far, as pockets of loan distress have lingered in the office, multifamily, and life science sectors, pressuring book values and stretching dividend coverage. Franklin BSP Realty (FBRT) – which focuses primarily on the typically steady multifamily space – dipped 13% this week after it replaced its CEO and announced a 43% reduction in its quarterly dividend. While its book value was relatively steady in Q4 – down 1% from Q3 – FBRT reported that its quantity of nonperforming loans in its core portfolio jumped from 3 to 7 in the quarter, including the foreclosure on a 272-unit apartment complex in Fort Worth, TX. FBRT noted that the dividend "reset" was driven by several factors:

...the recent declines in SOFR, the timing of our originations and repayments, and the overall size of our loan portfolio. In addition, spreads are at multi-decade tights, and REO liquidations are taking longer than originally anticipated.

The soft results dragged on its yet-to-report multifamily-focused peers as well, including Ready Capital (RC), which dipped more than 10% on the week. KKR Real Estate (KREF) was also under pressure this week after reporting similarly soft results with a pair of downgrades on a Cambridge life science property and a San Diego multifamily property and noted that its dividend policy was under review. Results from Blackstone Mortgage (BXMT) this week were notably stronger, however, with no significant incremental loan distress issues reported by either lender. BXMT highlighted 99% performance in its loan portfolio, up from from 96% in Q3.

2026 YTD Performance & 2025 Recap

Through the six weeks of 2026, REITs have outperformed the large-cap benchmarks by a sizable margin, driven by a broader value rotation that has buoyed the small- and mid-cap segment. The Equity REIT Index has posted gains of 6.9% this year, the Mortgage REIT Index has gained 2.9%, and the Housing 100 Index has rallied 9.1%. This compares to the fractional declines for the S&P 500, the more impressive 7.9% gain for the Mid-Cap 400, and the 8.9% gain for the Small-Cap 600. Within the REIT sector, 16 of 20 property sectors are higher on the year, led by Farmland, Data Center, and Net Lease REITs. At 4.05%, the 10-Year Treasury Yield is now lower by 11 basis points on the year, while the 2-Year Treasury Yield is lower by 7 basis points at 3.41%. Rates at the longest end of the yield curve have pulled back more significantly, with the 30-Year Treasury Yield lower by 15 basis points to 4.70%. The aggregate Bloomberg US Bond Index is higher by 1.3% this year after notching gains of 7% in 2025. In commodities markets, WTI Crude Oil has rebounded by 8% this year to around $61/barrel after dipping 12% in 2025. Gold has rallied nearly 16% after soaring by over 60% last year, while Bitcoin has dipped 20% after declining 6% in 2025.

Looking back on 2025, real estate equities significantly lagged the major equity benchmarks, notching a fourth straight year of underperformance versus all the major equity benchmarks - the S&P 500, S&P 400, and S&P 600 - for the first time ever. For REITs - the most interest rate-sensitive market segment – the underperformance aligns with notable weakness in ultra-long duration assets amid lingering inflation uncertainty. The REIT sector finished in the basement among the ten major asset classes - its second time in the bottom spot in the past four years and the third time in the past six years. During the preceding decade from 2010–2019, the REIT sector had never finished worse than sixth among the ten major asset classes. As discussed in our State of REIT Nation (available to our Investing Group subscribers), REITs have endured a brutal three-plus-year stretch since March 2022, underperforming the S&P 500 by over 70 percentage points, a magnitude that's more than twice as significant as the underperformance gap seen during the 2007-2009 Global Financial Crisis.

Economic Calendar In The Week Ahead

Following a hectic week of major economic reports, we'll see another busy slate of economic data in the holiday-shortened week ahead. The most closely watched reports come on Friday, beginning with the first look at fourth-quarter Gross Domestic Product. GDP data is expected to show that the U.S. economy grew at an annualized growth rate of 3% in the fourth quarter and for the full year, returning to a more normalized level following a Q1 and Q2–Q3 rebound on tariff distortions in import/export trends. We'll also see the delayed December PCE Price Index - the Fed's preferred inflation gauge – which is expected to show inflation rates hovering in the mid-to-high-2% range, down from the pandemic peak of 7%, but still above the Fed's stated 2.0% policy objective. Earlier in the week, on Tuesday, we'll see NAHB Homebuilder Confidence data, which is expected to show subdued sentiment in early 2026 despite the pullback in mortgage rates, with forecasts for a 22nd-straight month below the neutral 50-level. On Wednesday, we'll see the delayed Housing Starts report for both November and December, which are expected to show correspondingly sluggish construction activity in late 2025. The report is expected to confirm that total starts declined for a fourth straight year in 2025 following an early-pandemic boom in activity. On Thursday, we'll see Pending Home Sales for January, which are expected to show more upbeat recent trends in activity in the existing sales market as mortgage rates briefly dipped under 6% in late January before rebounding to the 6.10%-6.20% range in recent weeks. On Friday, we'll see the delayed New Home Sales report for both November and December. We'll also be watching the weekly employment metrics via ADP and Jobless Claims and a handful of regional and national PMI surveys throughout the week.

For an in-depth analysis of all real estate sectors, check out all of our quarterly reports: Apartments, Homebuilders, Manufactured Housing, Student Housing, Single-Family Rentals, Cell Towers, Casinos, Industrial, Data Centers, Malls, Healthcare, Net Lease, Shopping Centers, Hotels, Billboards, Office, Farmland, Storage, Timber, Mortgage, and Cannabis.
